assets.title: One process serves S3, one remembers names — the disk holds the truth
assets.summary: The web console, the S3 API server, the optional index service — and the plain directory (whole blobs in objects/, packed small blobs in volumes/) that is the whole database.
assets.description: Every moving part of the store: any S3 client (even the real aws CLI) talks to one Rust server, which asks a small index service which blob a key points at, and reads or writes plain files on disk. There is deliberately no database — the lesson of this project is that an object store is a layout discipline over a directory, not a service you call. Blob bytes land two ways: whole files under objects/, or — when packing is enabled — many small objects packed as needles into shared volumes/ files. Two background workers ride along: one ages objects out or compresses cold ones, the other re-hashes stored blobs to catch silent disk corruption before a reader does.

assets.title: Uploading a file: six steps from first byte to durable object
assets.summary: The PUT hot path — streamed chunk by chunk, hashed twice on the fly, committed with an atomic rename.
assets.description: What happens between a client sending a file and the store answering 200 OK. The body is never held in memory — each chunk is written to a temp file and fed to two hashes as it passes, so a 5 GB upload costs the same RAM as a 10 KB one. The object only appears in the final atomic rename, which is what makes a half-finished upload invisible instead of a corrupt file.

assets.title: Kill the power at any moment — what does a reader see afterwards?
assets.summary: The four write phases and their crash outcomes: the whole object or a 404, never a truncated file.
assets.description: The core durability lesson of the project. A write moves through four phases — stream to a temp file, fsync, atomic rename, index entry — and the diagram shows exactly what a crash inside each phase leaves behind. Because the bytes become durable before any name points at them, the worst a crash can do is strand cheap-to-clean garbage; it can never create a key that serves half a file.

assets.title: No folders, no copies — a key is only a pointer
assets.summary: The flat keyspace: listings fake the folders, identical content is stored once, delete just drops a pointer.
assets.description: S3's keyspace is flat — photos/2024/beach.jpg is one opaque string, and the folder view is computed on the fly by collapsing keys that share a prefix. Because blobs are named by the hash of their content, two keys with identical bytes share one blob on disk. That is why delete only removes the pointer: the bytes must survive until nothing references them, and a background garbage collector reclaims them later.

assets.title: How a one-byte edit re-uses most of a big file's bytes
assets.summary: Content-defined chunking cuts the upload at content-chosen boundaries, stores each chunk once by its hash, and records an ordered manifest — so near-identical files share every unchanged chunk.
assets.description: Whole-object dedup only ever shares byte-for-byte identical files, so a single changed byte costs a brand-new copy. With content-defined chunking (opt-in via CDC_ENABLED) the PUT body is split by a rolling hash of its own bytes — not at fixed offsets — so an edit shifts only the boundaries around it. Each chunk is SHA-256'd and committed once to the content-addressed store; an ordered manifest blob lists the chunk digests, and the index points the key at that manifest (the client still sees one object, with logical size, ETag, and Range). Upload a near-duplicate and only the edited chunk is new — every chunk after it keeps its digest and is shared on disk. GET reassembles the object by streaming its chunks back in order.

assets.title: Thousands of tiny files, a handful of big ones — how packing works
assets.summary: Small blobs are appended as 'needles' into shared append-only volume files and found by a RAM map of (volume, offset, size) — one open file for many objects, not one inode each.
assets.description: The small-file problem: a million unique 4 KiB thumbnails means a million filesystem inodes and a metadata lookup on every read. Haystack packing (opt-in via BLOB_LAYOUT) turns the Store into a facade over two physical backends. Small blobs are appended back-to-back as framed 'needles' into append-only volumes/N.dat files, and each one is located through a compact in-RAM map (digest → volume, offset, size) kept durable by a write-ahead log plus a periodic snapshot — that map is what replaces the per-object inode. A GET is then one map lookup and one ranged read; blobs too big to pack still fall back to one-file-each under objects/. Reclaiming the space held by deleted needles needs a compaction pass that isn't built yet.

assets.title: How a 5 GB upload survives a flaky network
assets.summary: Multipart: parallel, out-of-order, retryable parts — and the deliberately weird ETag that proves S3 compatibility.
assets.description: Big uploads are split into parts that arrive in parallel, in any order, and can be retried individually — a dropped connection costs one part, not the whole file. Completion validates every part against the client's MD5 receipts, stitches them together in order, and commits through the same atomic path as a normal upload. The resulting ETag follows S3's odd hash-of-hashes-plus-part-count formula — matching it exactly is what makes real AWS tools accept this store as S3.
